# Overview of Education


## Introduction

This chapter aims to establish the conceptual and contextual framework in which the
present work is situated, adopting a progressive approach from the general to the
particular.

We begin from the fundamental act of learning, the bedrock of all pedagogical
reflection, before examining how societies have structured this learning within formal
educational systems, drawing on the case of the Algerian system.

This progression will naturally lead us to the Final Year Project (PFE), the quintessential
synthesis exercise through which students mobilise all the competencies acquired during
their studies.

Finally, a critical analysis of the current management of PFE projects in Algerian
university institutions will illuminate the existing organisational shortcomings, thereby
constituting the starting point of the problem that motivates the present work.

## Learning

Learning constitutes one of the most fundamental activities of human beings. Since
prehistoric times, *Homo sapiens* has developed the capacity to accumulate, transmit, and
transform knowledge, enabling adaptation to varied environments and the construction of
complex civilisations. Unlike other species whose behaviour is essentially governed by
instinct, human beings depend largely on learning to acquire the skills necessary for their
survival and flourishing.

From the perspective of cognitive science, learning is defined as a process by which an
individual acquires new knowledge, skills, behaviours, or values through experience, study,
instruction, or practice [@anderson1982]. This process rests on complex neurological
mechanisms, notably synaptic plasticity and long-term memory, which allow the brain to
reconfigure itself in response to lived experiences [@anderson2000].

### Historical overview of education

The history of education in the West traces its roots to ancient Greece, with the
emergence of the first philosophical schools. Among the most celebrated, **Plato's Academy**
(founded around 387 BC) and **Aristotle's Lyceum** (founded in 335 BC, also known as the
Peripatetic school) laid the foundations of logical reasoning and systematic observation
[@lebrun2003]. At the Lyceum (@fig-aristote), Aristotle favoured an empirical and
encyclopaedic approach to knowledge, often teaching while walking through the gardens with
his disciples. @lynch1972

\vspace{1cm}

![Representation of Aristotle's school @lynch1972](/images/Schule_des_Aristoteles.jpg){#fig-aristote fig-align="center" width="50%"}

During the Middle Ages, teaching was structured around the seven liberal arts, before the
first universities emerged in the twelfth century, organised into faculties (Theology, Law,
Medicine, Arts) [@lebrun2003]. The modern era saw the emergence of Jesuit colleges and
royal teaching institutions such as the College de France. This period was marked by the
publication of influential pedagogical works such as that of Reixac in 1749 (@fig-reixac).

![Pedagogical work: *Instruccions per a l'ensenyança de minyons* (1749) @reixac1749](/images/Reixac.jpg){#fig-reixac fig-align="center" width="35%"}

**Finally**, the contemporary era marked the generalisation of compulsory and free
schooling in the nineteenth century, transforming education into a fundamental right and
a universal public service [@lebrun2003].


### Why do we learn?

The question of *why* we learn is both philosophical and pragmatic. Several fundamental
motivations explain the natural human drive to learn [@anderson2000]:

- **To succeed and flourish:** learning is the engine of personal and professional
  achievement. It enables individuals to reach their goals, overcome obstacles, and find
  their place in society.
- **To gain social recognition and qualifications:** in modern societies, certifications
  and diplomas constitute tangible proof of the knowledge and skills acquired. They
  condition access to employment, positions of responsibility, and social advancement.
- **To develop skills and adapt to the labour market:** in a world of perpetual
  technological and economic change, continuous training has become a necessity. Yesterday's
  skills can become obsolete within a few years, highlighting the importance of lifelong
  learning.
- **To contribute to collective progress:** beyond individual interest, learning nourishes
  scientific, technological, and cultural innovation. Each generation transmits and enriches
  the body of knowledge for the next.

These motivations justify the organisation, by societies, of formal educational systems
designed to structure and optimise the learning process from the earliest age.


## Education structure and training pathways

Formal education is the institutional mechanism by which societies organise the transfer
of knowledge and skills between generations. It is structured in successive stages, each
with its own pedagogical objectives, progressively guiding individuals from the acquisition
of foundational knowledge through to advanced professional specialisation.

In Algeria, as in the majority of countries, the educational system is organised in
several cycles defined by the Ministry of National Education and the Ministry of Higher
Education and Scientific Research (MESRS) [@loi9905]. This pathway, illustrated by
@fig-parcours-algerie, is divided into four major stages: primary education, middle
school, secondary education, and higher education. Each stage marks the student's success
with a diploma or national examination that conditions access to the next cycle.

![Educational pathway of a student (in Algeria) @france-edu-intl](/images/education.png){
    #fig-parcours-algerie
    fig-align="center"
    width="85%"
    fig-pos="H"
}

### Primary education

Primary education constitutes the first stage of compulsory schooling. In Algeria, it
spans five years (from approximately age 6 to 11) and covers the fundamentals: reading,
writing, arithmetic, natural sciences, and civic education [@loi9905]. The main objective
is to equip the child with the cognitive and social tools necessary for continuing
education. At the end of this cycle, pupils sit the **Primary School Leaving Examination**
and obtain the **Primary Studies Certificate** (CEP).

### Middle school education

The middle school cycle lasts four years in Algeria, covering grades 1AM through 4AM
[@loi9905]. It deepens and broadens the knowledge acquired in primary school, introducing
additional subjects such as advanced mathematics, physical sciences, history-geography,
and foreign languages. This cycle concludes with the **Middle Education Certificate (BEM)**,
a national examination that conditions access to high school.

### Secondary education

High school spans three years, covering grades 1AS through 3AS, and represents a pivotal
stage in the student's academic orientation [@loi9905]. Students specialise in streams
(sciences, humanities, mathematics, technology, management...) according to their
aptitudes and professional projects. This cycle ends with the **Baccalaureate**, a
national diploma granting access to higher education, often considered the student's first
official qualification.

### Higher education

Higher education represents the summit of formal academic training. In Algeria, it is
provided in universities, higher schools, institutes, and university centres spread
throughout the national territory [@loi9905]. Two major training systems coexist.

#### a) The LMD system (Licence - Master - Doctorate)

> The LMD system was implemented in Algeria under Executive Decree No. 04-371 of
> 21 November 2004. It aims, through an innovative framework, to integrate the University
> into economic development and to harmonise the higher education system with the rest of
> the world. [@univalger]

Introduced in Algeria from 2004-2005 as part of a reform aimed at harmonising the
Algerian university system with European standards (the Bologna Process) [@decret2004 ;
@berrouche2007], the LMD system is organised in three grades, as illustrated in @fig-lmd.
In this framework, a **credit** is the unit that measures the student's workload (lectures,
supervised tutorials, practical work, and personal study), equivalent to approximately
**20 to 25 hours of work per semester** [@mesrs2023 ; @mesrs2005guide]. Each semester
corresponds to 30 credits, and credits are **accumulative**: once validated, they are
permanently acquired, even in the event of repeating a year [@mesrs2005guide].

- **Licence (L):** the first university grade, obtained after three years (six semesters)
  following the Baccalaureate. The Licence validates **180 credits** and allows either
  direct entry into the workforce or continued study at Master's level [@mesrs2005guide].
- **Master (M):** the second grade, obtained after two additional years (four semesters)
  following the Licence. The Master validates **120 additional credits** and deepens
  specialisation. Two types exist: the academic Master (research-oriented) and the
  professional Master (workforce-oriented) [@mesrs2005guide].
- **Doctorate (D):** the third grade, accessible by competitive examination after obtaining
  the Master. It is awarded upon completion of an original research work of a minimum
  duration of three years and constitutes the highest academic grade [@decret2004].

![Structure of the LMD system in Algeria - semester progression and accumulative credits @mesrs2023](/images/lmd-timeline.png){#fig-lmd fig-align="center" width="80%"}

#### b) The engineering programme

Alongside the LMD system, several Algerian institutions offer a five-year engineering
programme following the Baccalaureate, awarding a **State Engineer's Diploma**
[@mesrs2004]. This programme is offered not only by the National Higher Schools (ENS)
and Polytechnic Schools, but also by certain universities such as USTHB (Houari Boumediene
University of Science and Technology) or the University of Blida. This curriculum is more
integrated and less modular than the LMD, with a national programme defined by the MESRS.
The training alternates between theoretical instruction, practical work, and industrial
internships, and concludes compulsorily with a Final Year Project [@insaniyat2018].

| Criterion | LMD System | Engineering Programme |
|---|---|---|
| Total duration | 3 years (Licence) + 2 years (Master) | 5 years |
| Degree awarded | Licence / Master | State Engineer's Diploma |
| Modularity | High (semesters, accumulative credits) | Low (integrated national programme) |
| Final validation | PFE module (Licence) / PFE semester (Master) | PFE (final semester) |
| Track | Academic or professional | Technical/professional |
| Entry requirements | Baccalaureate (all streams) | Baccalaureate (science streams) |

: Comparison between the LMD system and the engineering programme in Algeria [@decret2004 ; @mesrs2004] {tbl-colwidths="[30,35,35]"}


## Final Year Project (PFE)

At this stage of the academic journey, a natural question arises: how does a student
validate their cycle of studies? The answer lies in one key event: the Final Year Project,
or PFE.

In general, university curricula, whether at Licence or Master level, conclude with a
final year project. The PFE is an academic exercise that carries a final grade and marks
the end of a higher education cycle [@mesrs2004]. It is compulsory in *the majority* of
Licence programmes and in *all* Master programmes.

It represents for the student the opportunity to mobilise all the knowledge and
competencies acquired throughout their training in order to address a concrete problem,
whether scientific, technological, or organisational in nature, by producing a written
document (the **Thesis**) and generally an **applied component** of the solution, then
defending it before a jury.

The PFE is recognised internationally under various names: *Senior Thesis* (United States),
*Final Year Project* (United Kingdom), *Bachelor's Thesis* or *Master's Thesis*
(Switzerland and French-speaking countries). In Algeria, its place in the curriculum
varies according to the type of programme [@mesrs2004].

<!-- Here was the PFE section that I moved to chapter 2 -->

## Conclusion

This first chapter laid the theoretical and contextual foundations of our study. Starting
from the cognitive definition of learning [@anderson1982], we traced the historical
evolution of educational systems [@lebrun2003] before anchoring our analysis in the
Algerian context, governed by the 1999 framework law [@loi9905] and the 2004 LMD reform
[@decret2004]. We then focused our attention on the Final Year Project, whose regulatory
framework is defined by the MESRS [@mesrs2004 ; @mesrs2005guide]. The limitations
identified in its current management [@berrouche2007 ; @insaniyat2018] underscore the
importance of modernising this process through a dedicated digital solution, whose study
will be deepened in the following chapters.
